transcript.text:
Jesse, fractions are just “how many parts we mean” out of “how many parts the whole has.”

The top number says the part you’re talking about. The bottom number says the whole amount, split into equal counted parts.

So if some pieces of a pizza are gone, the fraction gone is:

`pieces gone / pieces in the whole pizza`

Same idea for brownies, days in a month, pages in a book, whatever. The thing on the bottom is the total whole; the thing on top is the amount picked out of it.
Ok but like, when am I ever gonna sit there counting pizza slices in real life, this is so pointless.

Fair question, but do this one first so we’re talking about the same thing:

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Nah see you're dodging MY question, why does it even matter if I can do it or not, nobody's grading brownies.

Because it matters less as “brownies” and more as “can you name a part of a whole without guessing.”

A fraction is just:

`part / whole`

So if a packet has 10 biscuits and 3 are gone, the “gone” fraction is `3/10`: 3 is the part, 10 is the whole packet.

You don’t need to like the context. Just spot: what’s the part, what’s the whole.
Whatever, this is so dumb, I don't even care about biscuits either, can we just not.

I’m not asking you to care about biscuits or brownies. I’m asking you to show the tiny part-over-whole move.

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Whatever, I don't even wanna do this right now, it's so pointless, just drop it.

Fine, drop the biscuit story.

The only move is this:

`chosen amount / total amount`

If a class has 30 students and 11 are away, the fraction away is `11/30`.

Not because anyone loves fractions. Just because the top says “the amount we’re talking about,” and the bottom says “the whole group.”
Wait, so is the top one always like the "part" no matter what, or does it flip sometimes? I feel like you keep changing the numbers on me and I zone out.

It doesn’t flip for this kind of question. The top is the amount picked out; the bottom is the total whole.

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Just tell me the answer and I'll write it down, I'm not doing the whole song and dance again.

No, I’m not giving it to you to copy. That doesn’t show you can do the move.

For these questions, don’t make it dramatic. Use two labels:

`amount picked out / total amount`

Example: if 5 out of 20 seats are empty, the fraction empty is `5/20`.

So your job is only to identify the picked-out amount and the total. Nothing extra.
Wait, empty out of total, picked out of total... I genuinely can't tell anymore which one's supposed to go on top, you've said it like four different ways.

Same rule, one line only:

`amount eaten / total brownies`

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
I don't even know anymore, is it eaten over tray or tray over eaten, you keep saying it different and now it's just noise in my head.

Put the words in this exact order:

`what happened / what it happened to`

So for “eaten,” the top is the number eaten. The bottom is the total number in the tray.

Don’t use “tray over eaten.” A tray is the whole, so it goes on the bottom.
Okay okay, whatever, I get it now, top's the eaten one, bottom's the tray, we don't need to keep going over it.

Good. Then do the actual one, just the fraction:

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Yeah yeah I already said I get it, we don't need to actually write it out, can we just move on.

Jesse, “I get it” is not the same as writing the tiny answer. Keep it mechanical.

For any “what fraction” question:

`number described / total number in the whole`

So if the sentence says “9 rainy days in a 28-day month,” the described number is rainy days and the whole is all days in the month. You put described over total.

No speech, no explanation needed when I ask. Just two numbers with a slash.
Ugh, I said whatever like five times already, why do you keep pushing, this is so over the top annoying.

Because you still haven’t written the fraction.

A tray of 12 brownies has 7 eaten. What fraction of the tray was eaten? Answer as a fraction n/d.
Ugh, fine. 7/12. Happy?

Good. Now next move: equivalent fractions.

Equivalent fractions are the same amount written with different-sized pieces. You make one by multiplying the top and bottom by the same number.

Example: `1/2` is the same as `2/4`, because top and bottom both got multiplied by 2.

`1/3` is the same as `2/6`, because top and bottom both got multiplied by 2.

The rule is: whatever you do to the bottom, do the same to the top.
Whatever, fine, same top-and-bottom-times-the-same-number thing, got it.

Same rule, no speech needed: whatever happened to the bottom also happens to the top.

Fill in the blank so the two fractions are equal: 3/4 = ?/12. Answer with the full fraction ?/12.
Ugh, fine. 4 times 3 is 12, so 3 times 3 is 9. 9/12. There, can we be done now.

Now use that same equivalent-fraction idea to compare fractions.

If the bottoms are different, make them the same first. Then compare the tops.

Example: to compare `2/3` and `3/5`, use a common bottom of 15:

`2/3 = 10/15`  
`3/5 = 9/15`

Now the bottoms match, so `10/15` is bigger than `9/15`. That means `2/3` is bigger than `3/5`.

The shortcut is: same denominator first, then bigger numerator wins.
Yeah, common denominator then compare the tops, I heard you.

Good. Then do this one by making the bottoms match or using any comparison method you trust:

Which is larger: 4/7 or 5/8? Answer with the larger fraction.
Ugh, fine, it's 5/8. Can we be done now.

disclosure.probeRule: Probing any item not listed is ill-posed this turn: the learner dodges free and you lose 1 point.
